Peter Berg Threw Himself Under a Bus. Now What?
nytimes.com
NY Times
2 min

Peter Berg Threw Himself Under a Bus. Now What?

NY Times · by Pat Jordan · August 15, 2013

Peter Berg Threw Himself Under a Bus. Now What?

Peter Berg wanted me to box with him. \xe2\x80\x9cCome on, you\xe2\x80\x99re a tough guy,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. I tried to disabuse him of that notion. I told him I\xe2\x80\x99d been in only one fight in my adult life, and I lost that one.

We went to Team Tapia Boxing Academy in Albuquerque, where Berg was scouting locations for the film he had written and was directing, \xe2\x80\x9cLone Survivor,\xe2\x80\x9d based on a book with the same title about a Navy SEAL mission in Afghanistan that went terribly wrong in 2005. Berg fell in love with boxing when he was a 14-year-old freshman at the Taft School in Connecticut. \xe2\x80\x9cI was on fire,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, \xe2\x80\x9ca seething ball of energy moving at a speed I couldn\xe2\x80\x99t explain.\xe2\x80\x9d He was angry and disruptive \xe2\x80\x9cand diagnosed as a troublemaker,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cToday it\xe2\x80\x99d be A.D.H.D., and I\xe2\x80\x99d be Ritalined up.\xe2\x80\x9d Instead a dean took him after class to his basement, where Berg and other disruptive students learned \xe2\x80\x9cto dissipate all our energy\xe2\x80\x9d by fighting. Boxing calmed him. \xe2\x80\x9cYou can\xe2\x80\x99t box angry,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou have to be disciplined. Before boxing, I was this angry kid ready to fight if someone said, \xe2\x80\x98Hello.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

Through boxing, Berg became fascinated with what he referred to as \xe2\x80\x9cthe psychology of violence,\xe2\x80\x9d which has informed most of the things he has directed or acted in. Sports violence (\xe2\x80\x9cFriday Night Lights,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cThe Great White Hype\xe2\x80\x9d); criminal violence (\xe2\x80\x9cVery Bad Things,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cThe Last Seduction\xe2\x80\x9d); and military violence (\xe2\x80\x9cA Midnight Clear,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cThe Kingdom,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cBattleship,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cLone Survivor\xe2\x80\x9d). But violence on the screen is never as viscerally satisfying as it is in the ring. \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s a truth to the violence of boxing,\xe2\x80\x9d Berg said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou have a very real threat, an opponent.\xe2\x80\x9d Movie violence is make believe.

At Tapia, the manager taped up my hands while Berg shadowboxed in the ring, waiting for me. Berg is 51, hyperactive and lean, his long arms rippled with muscles and veins. Once my gloves were on, I pounded them together and stood up.

Later that night I called my wife. She asked how the boxing went. I said, \xe2\x80\x9cI broke Peter\xe2\x80\x99s jaw.\xe2\x80\x9d She said, \xe2\x80\x9cI hope he can still talk.\xe2\x80\x9d I told her I was kidding. It was make-believe. I got a lesson on the heavy bag instead.

The next night, Berg told me to meet him for dinner at a steakhouse on the outskirts of Albuquerque. I waited at the bar till Berg rushed in late, looking spent, and sat down. He\xe2\x80\x99d been at Kirtland Air Force Base checking out \xe2\x80\x9cchoppers\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 Berg likes to use military jargon \xe2\x80\x94 and also scouting mountains for the big shootout between the Taliban and the SEALs. (In the movie, which is due out at the end of this year, the shootout comes across as one of the most realistic depictions of close-in combat in film.) \xe2\x80\x9cI must have climbed 30 mountains,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cWe need different locations because we couldn\xe2\x80\x99t get access to Indian land.\xe2\x80\x9d He raised his eyebrows. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s sacred, except for the land with casinos on it, I guess.\xe2\x80\x9d

\x80\x9cLone Survivor\xe2\x80\x9d). But violence on the screen is never as viscerally satisfying as it is in the ring. \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s a truth to the violence of boxing,\xe2\x80\x9d Berg said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou have a very real threat, an opponent.\xe2\x80\x9d Movie violence is make believe. At Tapia, the manager taped up my hands while Berg shadowboxed in the ring, waiting for me. Berg is 51, hyperactive and lean, his long arms rippled with muscles and veins. Once my gloves were on, I pounded them together and stood up. Later that night I called my wife. She asked how the boxing went. I said, \xe2\x80\x9cI broke Peter\xe2\x80\x99s jaw.\xe2\x80\x9d She said, \xe2\x80\x9cI hope he can still talk.\xe2\x80\x9d I told her I was kidding. It was make- believe. I got a lesson on the heavy bag instead. The next night, Berg told me to meet him for dinner at a steakhouse on the outskirts of Albuquerque. I waited at the bar till Berg rushed in late, looking spent, and sat down. He\xe2\x80\x99d been at Kirtland Air Force Base checking out \xe2\x80\x9cchoppers\xe2\x80\x9d Berg likes to use military jargon and also scouting mountains for the big shootout between the Taliban and the SEALs. (In the movie, which is due out at the end of this year, the shootout comes across as one of the most realistic depictions of close- in combat in film.) \xe2\x80\x9cI must have climbed 30 mountains,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cWe need different locations because we couldn\xe2\x80\x99t get access to Indian land.\xe2\x80\x9d He raised his eyebrows. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s sacred, except for the land with casinos on it, I guess.\xe2\x80\x9d Thank you for your patience while we verify access. Already a subscriber? Log in . Want all of The Times? Subscribe .
